market_rules:
The Sucre mayoral election is scheduled to take place in Bolivia on March 22, 2026.

This market will resolve according to the candidate who wins this election.

If the result of this election isn't known by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET, the market will resolve to "Other".

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the Bolivian electoral authority, the Tribunal Supremo Electoral (TSE) (https://www.oep.org.bo).

# Bolivians Enter a New Era of Popular Struggles
After a series of protests over cuts to fuel subsidies brought the country to a halt, Bolivia’s powerful social movements embark on a new chapter and face new forms of repression.
[Gabriel Rodríguez García] 
* January 28, 2026
*[Lea este artículo en español.] *
In late 2025 and early 2026, Bolivia’s cities and highways witnessed another wave of mass demonstrations, roadblocks, hunger strikes, and assemblies. Once again, state policy was overturned by street politics, popular mandates, and vetoes. On December 17, 2025, President Rodrigo Paz issued Supreme Decree 5503, which lifted fuel subsidies and reoriented economic policy toward neoliberalism. However, 24 days of protests forced the government to back down, although it managed to extract several key concessions from social movements.
This was the first major social conflict sincePaz came to power in October 2025. It will hardly be the last, as efforts to establish a new economic regime will face popular resistance. Bolivia is thus entering a new cycle of conflict marked by disputes over the way out of its brutal economic crisis—and the meaning of resistance itself.
## Tenuous Trust in the Government Evaporates
The 2025 elections had raised expectations for change. The victory of Paz and his vice president Edmand Lara, dependent upon[the support of] voters from the countryside and urban popular classes, served as a check on the openly radical right wing led by candidate Jorge Quiroga. However, after the runoff election, the populist campaign rhetoric dissipated and the alliance between Paz and Lara[fractured]. The vice president became an opposition figure and Paz responded by isolating him,[curtailing his pow

news.headline: Bolivia Set for Historic Presidential Election Runoff
news.content:
Bolivia Set for Historic Presidential Election Runoff

Mon 18th Aug, 2025

In a landmark development for Bolivian politics, the nation is preparing for an unprecedented presidential election runoff, a first in its democratic history. As the political landscape continues to evolve, candidates from centrist and right-wing factions are emerging as key contenders, signaling a potential shift in governance.

The electoral race has intensified, with voters poised to make critical decisions that could reshape the country's political framework. The runoff election is set to take place after no candidate secured the necessary majority in the initial round of voting, reflecting a divided electorate.

Political analysts suggest that this runoff may serve as a pivotal moment for Bolivia, potentially ushering in new policies and governance styles that differ from previous administrations. The centrist candidate has garnered significant support among the middle class and urban voters, while right-wing candidates are appealing to more conservative bases, particularly in rural areas.

The significance of this runoff extends beyond just electoral outcomes; it represents a broader trend of shifting political alliances in Latin America. Observers note that voter sentiment is increasingly influenced by economic conditions, social issues, and calls for transparency and reform.

As the campaign heats up, candidates are expected to focus on key issues such as economic recovery, social welfare, and national security, which resonate deeply with the electorate. Voter engagement is critical, as the outcome of this runoff could determine Bolivia's trajectory for years to come.

The implications of this runoff are being closely monitored by international stakeholders, as Bolivia's political stability is vital not only for the region but also for global economic interests. As the date approaches, the world watches with anticipation to see how Bolivians will exercise their democratic rights in th
